nodes.body: Fixed. I've updated the PRD to clarify that file_hash is used for content change detection at a given path.
Fixed. I've updated the scanner to robustly mark missing files as NOT_PRESENT even if they were already COMPLETED, and updated the docs to accurately describe this behavior.
Fixed. Added a mention that these constants should be imported from src/core/analyzer_names.py.
Fixed. I've updated the README.md to clarify that moved files are handled as a deletion followed by a new ingestion.
Fixed. Updated the PRD to clarify that file_hash is used for content change detection.
Fixed. I've updated the README.md to clarify that moved files are handled as a deletion followed by a new ingestion.
Fixed. I've updated the README to explicitly state that moved files are handled as a deletion followed by a new ingestion, rather than suggesting hash-based lookup persistence across moves.

Fixed. I've updated the FTS logic to automatically remove documents from the search index when they are marked as NOT_PRESENT. The scanner now triggers this cleanup during ingestion, and the PRD has been updated to reflect this behavior.

Fixed. I've updated the final pass in ingest_directory() to explicitly check os.path.exists(path) for any document not in processed_doc_ids. This ensures that documents are only marked as NOT_PRESENT if they are truly missing from the disk, preventing false positives when using filters or limits.
Fixed. I've updated the PR title and description to reflect that this PR now includes runtime behavior improvements for filesystem synchronization and FTS accuracy.
Fixed. Updated the early return path in ingest_directory() to return ([], []) to maintain type consistency.

Calling `sync_document_to_fts()` inside this startup loop commits the session for every missing document (and then the function commits again at the end). For large datasets this can cause significant lock contention and slow startup. Consider batching: mark all missing docs as `NOT_PRESENT`, commit once, then perform a single/batched FTS delete (or collect IDs and sync in a separate pass) to reduce per-row commits.

Tests were updated to unpack the new `(processed_ids, missing_ids)` return type, but there’s no assertion coverage for the new `missing_ids` behavior (e.g., missing-on-disk docs returned/marked, and importantly that non-processed docs due to `limit`/filters/ignored types are *not* treated as missing). Adding a focused test for the final “mark missing docs” pass would help prevent regressions in filesystem synchronization.

This loops over `missing_ids` and calls `sync_document_to_fts()` per document, which performs its own `DELETE` + `COMMIT` under the FTS semaphore. If many documents are missing, this will be slow and increase SQLite lock churn. Consider a bulk delete path for missing docs (e.g., one `DELETE ... WHERE rowid IN (...)` under the semaphore, then a single commit) or a dedicated `sync_missing_documents_to_fts(missing_ids)` helper.

`ingest_directory()` is annotated to return `Tuple[List[int], List[int]]`, but there are still code paths that return a bare list (e.g., the early invalid-directory branch). That will break callers that unpack the result. Please ensure *every* return path returns a 2-tuple (such as `([], [])`).

The “mark missing docs” pass treats `doc.id not in processed_doc_ids` as “missing on disk”. That’s not equivalent: docs can be skipped due to `limit`, `mime_type_filter`, `IGNORED_EXTENSIONS`, or `IGNORED_MIME_TYPES` while still existing on disk, and would be incorrectly marked `NOT_PRESENT`. Fix by explicitly checking filesystem presence (e.g., `os.path.exists(doc.path)` / tracking a `seen_paths` set from the walk) before marking NOT_PRESENT, and consider skipping this pass when running with `limit`/filters if you can’t prove completeness.

`ingest_directory()` is now typed as returning `(processed_ids, missing_ids)`, but the invalid-directory branch still returns a bare list (`return []`). Any caller that unpacks the return value (tests/UI do) will raise a `ValueError`. Return a 2-tuple consistently in this branch (e.g., `([], [])`).

The “mark missing docs” pass uses `doc.id not in processed_doc_ids` as a proxy for “missing on disk”, but docs can be absent from `processed_doc_ids` due to `limit`, `mime_type_filter`, ignored MIME/extensions, or an ingestion error—while still existing on disk. This will incorrectly mark present files as `NOT_PRESENT`. Instead, explicitly check filesystem existence (e.g., `os.path.exists(doc.path)`), and consider tracking `seen_paths` during the walk to avoid false positives.

In the missing-docs loop, `doc.id not in processed_doc_ids` does a linear scan of a list for every DB doc, which can become O(N^2) on large libraries. Convert `processed_doc_ids` to a `set` once before the loop (and precompute `base_path_resolved = base_path.resolve()` outside the loop) to keep this pass fast.

This sync loop calls `sync_document_to_fts()` once per missing document, and `sync_document_to_fts()` commits each time. For many deletions this will be slow and create extra SQLite write contention. Prefer a single bulk delete of `document_fts` rows for all `missing_ids` (and one commit), or refactor `sync_document_to_fts()` to support batch operations / deferred commit.

These exception handlers log with `logger.error(... {e})`, which drops the traceback and makes failures harder to debug in production. Use `logger.exception(...)` (or `logger.error(..., exc_info=True)`) so the stack trace is captured.

The new “final pass” missing-file reconciliation in `ingest_directory()` doesn’t appear to be covered by tests (existing tests cover the startup helper `_load_and_queue_existing_docs`, but not the ingestion-time NOT_PRESENT marking). Add a test that ingests a directory, deletes/moves one file, re-runs `ingest_directory()`, and asserts the corresponding Document is marked `NOT_PRESENT` (and that existing-on-disk but filtered/limited files are not).

`ingest_directory()` is annotated to return `Tuple[List[int], List[int]]`, but the invalid/non-directory early-return branch still returns a bare list (`return []` at line 182). Callers that unpack (as the UI/tests now do) will crash on this path. Return a 2-tuple consistently in all exit paths (e.g., empty processed + empty missing).

The “mark missing docs” pass equates “not in processed_doc_ids” with “missing on disk”. That’s not correct: docs can be skipped due to `limit`, `mime_type_filter`, or ignored MIME types, and would be incorrectly marked `NOT_PRESENT` even if the file still exists. This pass should explicitly check on-disk existence (or track a `seen_paths`/`seen_doc_ids` set from the walk) before setting `NOT_PRESENT`.

Syncing each `missing_id` to FTS one-by-one results in N separate DELETEs + commits (because `sync_document_to_fts()` commits). For large directories with many deletions, this can make ingestion completion noticeably slower. Consider batching the FTS cleanup into a single `DELETE ... WHERE rowid IN (...)` (or otherwise committing once) rather than looping per-ID.

In startup reconciliation, missing files call `sync_document_to_fts()` inside the per-document loop. Since `sync_document_to_fts()` performs a commit, this can cause many small transactions/commits on startup when lots of files are missing (and there’s also an extra `await session.commit()` after the loop). Consider batching FTS deletions and committing once for the whole reconciliation pass.

The “mark missing docs” pass treats `doc.id not in processed_doc_ids` as equivalent to “missing on disk”. That’s not true when `limit` is hit, when `mime_type_filter` is used, when files are ignored by extension/MIME, or when ingestion errors occur—those docs would be incorrectly marked `NOT_PRESENT`. Track which paths were actually seen during `os.walk` (e.g., a `seen_paths` set) and/or explicitly check `os.path.exists(doc.path)` before marking NOT_PRESENT; also consider skipping this pass when running with `limit`/filters.

`missing_ids` are synced to FTS one-by-one via `sync_document_to_fts()`, which performs its own SELECT/DELETE and commits each time. For large directories with many deletions, this will be slow and create extra SQLite write contention. Consider batching missing-doc FTS removals (single `DELETE ... WHERE rowid IN (...)` + single commit) or adding a dedicated bulk helper for FTS cleanup.

New behavior (returning `missing_doc_ids` and marking DB rows as `NOT_PRESENT` during ingestion) doesn’t appear to be covered by tests yet. Add a test that deletes/moves a previously ingested file and asserts (1) it’s returned in `missing_doc_ids` and (2) its `Document.status` becomes `NOT_PRESENT` (including when the doc was previously `COMPLETED`).

`ingest_directory()` is now typed to return `Tuple[List[int], List[int]]`, but the invalid/non-directory branch still returns a bare list (`return []`). Any caller that unpacks the result (as tests/UI now do) will crash. Return a 2-tuple consistently in this branch (e.g., `([], [])`), and ensure any other early-return branches do the same.
